title: "Domain-Scoped Venture Catalogues"
type: standard
owner: tim
solve_for: >
  Ventures need to track things that matter — technologies, vendors,
  processes, team structures — without the overhead of full governance.
  Without a defined catalogue standard, ventures either skip tracking
  (creating blind spots) or over-govern (creating compliance fatigue).
  JOS must define what a catalogue looks like, what each domain requires,
  and how catalogue existence is verified.

**Framework Note:** This standard's content is fully covered by the Holonic Venture Decomposition Framework (JOS-FW-001), §5 (Five Boundary Contracts), §7 (Templates and Instances), §8 (Slots and Boundaries), and §10 (Three Levels of Utility). This object is retained as a teaching entry point and historical record.

# Domain-Scoped Venture Catalogues

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Standard

Every venture SHOULD maintain domain-scoped catalogues listing the items
it needs to know about but does not individually govern. Catalogues use
a lighter schema than governed components — no solve_for, no review_cadence
per entry — but are themselves governed as a class. The practice of
cataloguing is mandatory for adopted domains; what goes into each
catalogue entry is the venture's responsibility.

## The Three-Tier Awareness Model

This standard formalizes the middle tier of organizational awareness
established by JOS-D0046:

| Tier | What It Means | JOS Overhead |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| **Governed** | Full frontmatter, owned, reviewed, stage-labeled | Full — solve_for, review_cadence, registry entry |
| **Catalogued** | Listed, typed, discoverable, may have an owner | Light — id, name, type, owner, status |
| **Unknown** | Not tracked anywhere | None — invisible |

JOS exists to eliminate the Unknown tier. This standard provides the
tools for the Catalogued tier so ventures don't have to choose between
full governance and nothing.

## Key Principle: Governance Flows to the Class, Not the Instance

Individual catalogue entries are not governed components. The practice of
cataloguing IS governed. "Every venture must catalogue its technologies"
is a JOS standard (this document). "Technology X must have a review
cadence" is NOT — that is per-instance overhead applied to something
that only needs to be known.

This means:
- The catalogue schema is JOS-level (governed, defined here)
- The catalogue content is venture-level (local, maintained by the venture)
- The existence of the catalogue is enforceable via charter and gates
- The completeness of catalogue entries is the venture's responsibility

## Minimum Catalogue Schema

Every catalogue entry must include at minimum:

```yaml
- id: "<VENTURE-PREFIX>-<TYPE>-###"   # Venture-prefixed ID
  name: "<human-readable name>"       # What it is
  type: "<catalogue type>"            # technology, vendor, process, role, etc.
  owner: "<person or team>"           # Who is responsible
  status: "active | deprecated | planned"
  domain: "<primary domain>"          # Which governance domain this falls under
```

Optional fields that ventures may add per catalogue type:

```yaml
# Technology extension
  version: "<current version>"
  vendor: "<provider>"
  purpose: "<one-line what it does>"
  dependencies: ["<other-id>", ...]

# Vendor extension
  contract_end: "<date>"
  annual_cost: "<amount>"
  primary_contact: "<person>"

# Process extension
  frequency: "daily | weekly | monthly | ad-hoc"
  sop_ref: "<link to SOP if exists>"
  automation_level: "manual | semi | full"

# Role extension
  reports_to: "<person or role>"
  headcount: <number>
  hiring_status: "filled | open | planned"
```

The schema is extensible. This standard defines the minimum; ventures
add fields their context requires. Per JOS-R05 (Universal Viewability),
catalogue files must be in a parseable, non-proprietary format — YAML
is recommended but not mandated.

## Domain-Scoped Catalogue Expectations

When a venture adopts a governance domain (via `adopted_domains` in the
charter), it commits to maintaining the catalogue for that domain's
scope. Each domain carries expectations for what should be catalogued:

| Domain | Code | Catalogue Scope |
|--------|------|----------------|
| Governance of Governance | meta | Governed components, governance decisions, standing orders |
| Engineering & Development | eng | Technologies, APIs, dependencies, deployment targets, system architecture |
| Documentation & Knowledge | doc | Documentation repositories, knowledge bases, reference materials, templates |
| Data & Analytics | dat | Data sources, pipelines, storage locations, schemas, retention policies |
| Finance & Economics | fin | Revenue streams, cost centers, vendor contracts, billing relationships |
| Strategy & Planning | str | Strategic initiatives, OKRs/goals, competitive positioning, market segments |
| Operations & Process | ops | Processes, SOPs, vendor relationships, tools, service agreements |
| People & Organization | ppl | Team structure, roles, responsibilities, skill inventories, hiring pipelines |
| Legal & Compliance | leg | Contracts, compliance requirements, regulatory obligations, IP inventory |

These expectations are illustrative, not exhaustive. Each venture
determines what its domain catalogues contain beyond the floor. A
venture that has adopted `eng` must have a technology catalogue; what
fields that catalogue has beyond the minimum schema is the venture's
choice.

## Catalogue Organization

Ventures with sufficient complexity should organize catalogue entries
using the systems/subsystems pattern (per JOS-D0042), but this is
recommended, not mandated:

- **Small ventures** (< 10 entries per domain): flat list is fine.
- **Medium ventures** (10-50 entries): group into logical systems.
- **Large ventures** (50+ entries): systems with subsystems.

An optional `system` field in the catalogue schema supports this
grouping without requiring it.

## Catalogue Location

Each venture declares its catalogue location in the charter:

```yaml
venture_config:
  catalogue_path: "docs/catalogue/"
```

The catalogue may be stored as:
- A directory of YAML files (one per domain or per system)
- A single YAML file with sections
- A database with a YAML export for discoverability
- Any format meeting JOS-R05 (Universal Viewability)

JOS cares that the catalogue exists and is discoverable, not how it is
stored internally.

## What Good Looks Like

- A new participant can find the venture's catalogue within 30 seconds
  via the charter's `catalogue_path` declaration.
- Every adopted domain has corresponding catalogue entries — no blind
  spots for adopted domains.
- Catalogue entries have enough metadata for triage (id, name, type,
  owner, status at minimum).
- Catalogues are machine-readable (YAML, JSON, CSV) as well as
  human-readable.
- The catalogue is maintained — `deprecated` items are marked, not
  silently removed; `planned` items have owners.
- The catalogue complements, not duplicates, governed components — items
  that warrant full governance are promoted, not double-tracked.

## Benchmark Floors

| Metric | Floor | Notes |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Domain coverage | 100% of adopted domains have a catalogue file | Empty catalogue is a gap, not compliance |
| Entry completeness | Every entry has all 6 minimum fields | id, name, type, owner, status, domain |
| Freshness | Catalogue reviewed at least quarterly | Not per-entry review — catalogue-level check |
| Charter declaration | `catalogue_path` present in charter | Discoverability requires declaration |

## What This Standard Does NOT Do

- **Does not mandate specific tools.** Ventures pick their own storage
  format. JOS cares about discoverability, not implementation.
- **Does not create per-instance governance.** Catalogue entries are not
  governed components. No solve_for, no review_cadence per entry.
- **Does not mandate systems/subsystems.** That organizational pattern
  is recommended for complex ventures, not required for all.
- **Does not replace governed registries.** The `.jos/registry.yaml`
  pattern (JOS-S01) is for governed components. Catalogues are for
  everything else worth knowing about.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-D0046 (Catalogue Standard and Best Practices Model) — the
  decision this standard implements
- JOS-D0040 (9 Governance Domains) — the domains that scope catalogues
- JOS-D0042 (Systems/Subsystems Orthogonality) — optional organizational
  pattern for catalogue entries
- JOS-S01 (Discoverable Registries) — the governed registry pattern
  that catalogues extend to lighter-weight items
- JOS-D0022 (Pit of Success) — inspiration docs make cataloguing easy
- JOS-D0044 (Context Bundle) — catalogues complement the identity layer
- JOS-D0045 (Venture Ecosystem Schema) — catalogues are part of
  venture structure
